costs: Continued unpredictability for families and staff planning
content.equity_impact: Negative/neutral: perpetuates existing inequity because school survival depends on local social capital and political salience rather than need, disadvantaging villages with weaker mobilization capacity, including many with higher Roma population shares.
expected_benefits: No new administrative or fiscal burden created
Preserves existing informal channels that have occasionally saved schools (e.g., 2025 Budapest reversal)
content.goal: Describe the baseline trajectory if no new statutory framework is adopted: village schools persist only as long as enrollment and district-center discretion allow, with survival determined by informal local mobilization rather than rule-based criteria.

implementation_steps.action: Maintain current administrative practice without introducing new thresholds, transport guarantees, or consultation rules
Continue ad hoc mobilization (petitions, protests, NGO support) as the only available lever against closure
implementation_steps.timeline: Reactive, triggered by individual closure proposals

mechanisms: Closure decisions continue to be made administratively by school district centers citing enrollment/cost criteria without a dedicated statutory procedure for demographic decline (F-live-legal_and_governance-04).

Closure/merger decisions are occasionally reversed via informal political-administrative intervention after public protest, not through codified appeal (F-live-legal_and_governance-08, F-live-political_feasibility-07).
Local social capital (bonding/bridging networks) determines survival unevenly across villages with similar demographics (F-live-portuguese_reform-10, F-live-political_feasibility-03).
Structural fiscal pressure from national class-size/per-pupil funding formulas persists regardless of governance form (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04).
risks: Uneven outcomes across villages with similar demographics purely due to differential social capital (F-live-portuguese_reform-10)
Continued territorial equity gaps as 38% of settlements already lack any public educational institution (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06)
Persistent contested segregation dynamics via church-sector growth continuing unchecked (F-live-finnish_reform-09, F-live-demography-08)
content.title: Status-quo continuation with informal ad hoc negotiation (passive-change counterfactual)
